items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.

items.q: What is the difference between a wood stain and a varnish?
items.correct: Stain colours the wood; varnish provides a protective surface coat.
items.random_wrong: Stain is permanent, varnish always peels off.
items.why: Students confuse colouring (stain penetrates) with protecting (varnish sits on top). Many products combine both, but their primary functions differ. Often stain is applied first, then varnish.

items.q: What is the difference between ductility and malleability?
items.correct: Ductility is being drawn into wires; malleability is being hammered into sheets.
items.misconception: They mean exactly the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Ductility is for metals, malleability is for plastics.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms because both involve deformation. Ductility = tensile (stretching into wires); malleability = compressive (rolling/hammering into sheets). Some metals excel at one but not the other.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: It's UK Design Council. “IDEO” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: It's SCAMPER. “TRIZ” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 diamonds. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
